published_date: 2026-04-27
video_id: 0gtKKoo4XM0
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0gtKKoo4XM0
title: The Macro Minute: Is it time for investors to take profits?

Happy Monday out there team 42, it's
your skipper here Darius Dale to present
our macro minute for Monday, April 27th,
2026. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lead off
morning note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, is it
time for investors to take profits? Our
answer is from a super short-term
perspective, this is not a bad idea.
This is a crucial week for corporate
reporting. Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, and
Microsoft, accounting for roughly 25% of
S&P 500 market cap, report on Wednesday,
followed by Apple on Thursday.
Regardless of how good or bad the
results are, the hurdles for sales and
earnings beats and revisions to guidance
are so high that investors should brace
for profit taking flows. Moreover, this
is a crucial week for major central bank
activity. On Tuesday, we have the Bank
of Japan, on Wednesday, we have the
Brazil Central Bank, Bank of Canada, and
then the Fed, followed by the Bank of
England and the European Central Bank on
Thursday. Investors should brace for
directionally hawkish forward guidance
that could spur profit taking flows as
well. As always, I'll wrap up with the
question from our community. This one's
titled Around the Horn. It says, "Thank
you for an amazing Around the Horn
webcast at EED. It's great to be back
and it's always a pleasure to hear you
talking with so much professionality and
passion on what you're building here."
Thanks for the kind feedback. Quick
question, in order for source of funds
to start working, does it mean that
these mega cap AI providers will start
to lag underperform in order for capital
to be to be reallocated into AI
beneficiaries? Basically, are you
talking in a way uh uh uh uh uh uh how
breadth is going to start broadening in
a more structural manner, hence AI
providers will simply stall their
overperformance? Thanks for the hard
work." So, I think the question is
getting at a very important discussion
in one of our uh uh uh uh uh uh uh uh uh
uh active research themes, which is
titled Source of Funds, which is
essentially calling for a reversal of
the structural outperformance of, you
know, AI providers, you know, tech
consumer uh communication services
companies relative to the broader index.
You know, our core thesis on AI from the
perspective of AI diffusion is that
we're essentially transferring
technology that has allowed these tech
companies to have, you know,
structurally elevated uh gross profit
margins, structurally elevated uh free
cash flow dynamics relative to other
businesses and other industries. Well,
they're essentially transferring that
same technology that's given them a
competitive advantage and given it to
everyone else. And so, what we should
see over time is a convergence in the
productivity, a convergence in the
margins, a convergence in the
profitability, and ultimately a
convergence in the valuation between,
you know, the AI providers and the AI uh
uh uh uh uh adopters across other
industries and across other geographies
as well. And this is something we've
seen in recent quarters in terms of the
sustained outperformance of
international equities relative to US
equities. Just kind of wrapping up on
this,
if you look at a chart of tech and
communication services valuations and
market cap, we can see that tech and
communication services valuations are
pretty much back on a trailing 12-month
PE basis and trailing 12-month price to
sales basis, we're pretty much back to
where we were prior to the dot-com
bubble bursting, and we're well north of
the price to sales multiple that we saw
prior to the dot-com bubble bursting.
And then, if you look at tech and comm
services as a percent of S&P 500 market
cap at 46% of the index, I mean, we're
in no man's land relative certainly
relative to the dot-com bubble, where we
peaked out at around, you know, just shy
of 40% or roughly around 40%. So, in our
opinion, we think the transfer of this
technology and all the productivity
enhancing benefits of the technology
from these companies to the rest of the
companies in the index and the rest of
the companies in the world is ultimately
going to cause a convergence in
productivity, margins, profitability,
and ultimately valuations. And in our
view, this is going to be a multi-year
uh trade, you know, consistent with an
unwind of the US's net international
investment uh deficit. So, we'll wrap it
up there. Darius Dale here presenting
our macro minute for Monday, April 27th,
2026. That's the wrap up there today.
We'll catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
